coredata.dc:description: The present study reports the synthesis, photocatalytic decolorization of reactive black 5 dye and phytotoxicity of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and iron co-doped TiO2 photocatalysts via modified sol gel method. GQDs were synthesized by direct pyrolysis of citric acid (CA). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and energy dispersion spectroscopy (EDS), Raman spectroscopy, atomic force microscopy (AFM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS), Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) and photoluminescence spectroscopy (PL) were used to determine the physicochemical properties of the best performing photocatalysts. The results indicated improved physicochemical properties of GQD-0.1Fe-TiO2-300 with root mean square roughness (Rz) (33.82 nm), higher surface area (170.79 m2 g−1), pore volume (0.08 cm3 g−1), and bandgap (2.94 eV). Moreover, GQD-0.1Fe co-doping of TiO2 greatly improved the photocatalytic decolorization efficiency for RB5 dye. The photocatalytic reaction followed the pseudo first order reaction with gradual decrease in Kapp values for increment in RB5 concentration. The KC value was obtained as 2.45 mg L−1 min−1 while the KLH value was 0.45 L mg−1 indicating the heterogeneous reaction system followed the Langmuir-Hinshelwood isotherm and simultaneously occurring adsorption and photocatalytic processes. Photocatalytic reaction mechanism studies exhibited the holes and OH radicals as the main active species in the GQD-0.1Fe-TiO2-300 responsible for the decolorization of RB5. The proposed reaction pathway showed that both Fe-TiO2 and GQDs play important role in generation of electrons and holes. Additionally, GQD-0.1Fe-TiO2-300 were durable up to four cycles. Phytotoxicity assay displayed that treated water and best performing photocatalysts had no effect on Lycopersicon esculentum seed germination. Therefore, the proposed system can pave a viable solution for safe usage of dye loaded wastewater and effluent for irrigation after treatment.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The present study reports the synthesis, photocatalytic decolorization of reactive black 5 dye and phytotoxicity of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and iron co-doped TiO2 photocatalysts via modified sol gel method. GQDs were synthesized by direct pyrolysis of citric acid (CA). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and energy dispersion spectroscopy (EDS), Raman spectroscopy, atomic force microscopy (AFM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS), Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) and photoluminescence spectroscopy (PL) were used to determine the physicochemical properties of the best performing photocatalysts. The results indicated improved physicochemical properties of GQD-0.1Fe-TiO2-300 with root mean square roughness (Rz) (33.82 nm), higher surface area (170.79 m2 g−1), pore volume (0.08 cm3 g−1), and bandgap (2.94 eV). Moreover, GQD-0.1Fe co-doping of TiO2 greatly improved the photocatalytic decolorization efficiency for RB5 dye. The photocatalytic reaction followed the pseudo first order reaction with gradual decrease in Kapp values for increment in RB5 concentration. The KC value was obtained as 2.45 mg L−1 min−1 while the KLH value was 0.45 L mg−1 indicating the heterogeneous reaction system followed the Langmuir-Hinshelwood isotherm and simultaneously occurring adsorption and photocatalytic processes. Photocatalytic reaction mechanism studies exhibited the holes and OH radicals as the main active species in the GQD-0.1Fe-TiO2-300 responsible for the decolorization of RB5. The proposed reaction pathway showed that both Fe-TiO2 and GQDs play important role in generation of electrons and holes. Additionally, GQD-0.1Fe-TiO2-300 were durable up to four cycles. Phytotoxicity assay displayed that treated water and best performing photocatalysts had no effect on Lycopersicon esculentum seed germination. Therefore, the proposed system can pave a viable solution for safe usage of dye loaded wastewater and effluent for irrigation after treatment.

ce:section.ce:para: The textile industry is a prominent consumer of freshwater, whereby dyes and other chemicals are used in various stages of fabric processing. Among various contaminants, colorants are unquestionably regarded as the key pollutants in textile wastewater (Khan et al., 2020). From both economic and employment viewpoints, the textile industry is categorized as one of the world's largest and most important industries. The share of Pakistani textiles account for less than 5% of total global textile exports (Memon et al., 2020). Despite these economic advantages, aquatic ecosystem is facing tremendous pressure of unspent toxic dyes as almost 15%–50% of reactive dyes remain unutilized during dyeing processes of fabric and eventually end up in the freshwater environment (Shah et al., 2020). Among different reactive azo dyes, reactive black 5 (RB5), is widely used dye in the textile industry due to its relatively low cost, low energy consumption, bright color and high stability which ranks it as an attractive option for the coloring industry (Silva et al., 2018). RB5 is an anionic dye with two azo groups, the anionic character of RB5 is linked with the presence of sulfonated group in the structure (Sarvestani and Doroudi, 2020). Dyes cause the interruption to aquatic life through creating barrier to light penetration and reoxygenation of water environment thereby affecting the phytoplankton growth (Ismail et al., 2019) and dyes loaded wastewaters have been marked as the source of mutagenic activity (Lima et al., 2007) and high concentration of RB5 dye induce teratogenic abnormalities in zebrafish embryos (Manimaran et al., 2018). Moreover, RB5 affects negatively the germination rates, and overall plant growth (Cicek et al., 2012; Shafqat et al., 2017). Dyes are ranked an important class of pollutants besides other organic pollutants like polyaromatic hydrocarbons (Iqbal et al., 2019) and polychlorinated biphenyls and their mutagenic and carcinogenic concerns in living organisms and their long-term retention is soil colloids and presence around 12.3–456.2 mg kg−1 soil of azo dyes may interfere with plants germination (Mahmood et al., 2017; Weisburger, 2002; Zhou, 2001). Therefore, it is imperative to treat textile wastewater loaded with azo dyes prior its dumping to freshwater streams and its subsequent usage in irrigation.

Some conventional chemical and physical methods are applied in textile industry to remove dyes from wastewater, but these methods are not effective in total mineralization of dyes (Aliabadi and Mahmoodi, 2018; Hamad et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2018). Different techniques like adsorption (Haroon et al., 2020; Rehman et al., 2017; Shah et al., 2020), coagulation and flocculation (Dotto et al., 2019), electrochemical processes (Zazou et al., 2019), membrane technology (Leaper et al., 2019) and aerobic and anaerobic treatments (Rathour et al., 2019) are available for the treatment of textile wastewater. The chemical treatment methods for the abatement of RB5 dye include Fenton and photo-Fenton process (Meriç et al., 2004; Yuranova et al., 2004). Most of current techniques are not proficient enough to effectively decolorize toxic dyes and to pursue the imposition of new and/or tighter regulations concerning the discharge of wastewater in many countries. Therefore, search for new technology for the treatment of azo dyes loaded effluents gets centric position to tackle environmental safety concerns.

Various nanomaterials have been studied for environmental (Altaf et al., 2021) and agricultural applications (Arshad et al., 2021; Waani et al., 2021), and wastewater treatment (Khan et al., 2021). Upadhyay and coworkers studied the combined role of ZnO:TiO2:CdO in advance oxidation processes and reported the optimum efficiency 98% and 96%, respectively, for methyl orange (MO) and methylene blue (MB) dyes with reaction time 40 min (Upadhyay et al., 2020). Ferrate (VI) was also ranked as an alternative and effective (98.2% removal) oxidizing agent for Congo red dye (Fatimah et al., 2021). Moreover, Gadolinium oxide (Gd2O3) showed better decolorization for different azo dyes in order of methyl orange>acid orange7 >acid yellow23 (Jeon et al., 2021). However, photocatalysis through TiO2 or modified TiO2 semiconductor is gaining attraction due to its low toxicity, inexpensiveness, non-selectiveness of contaminants and rapid reaction. The main limitation to widespread photocatalysis application is associated to the wide bandgap of TiO2 (3.0–3.2 eV). Various metals (V, W, Cu) and nonmetal (N, C) doping to TiO2 have been carried out to improve its photocatalytic abatement capability for environmental pollutants, especially in wastewater (Adly et al., 2019; El Mragui, 2019; Khan, 2014; Pourzad et al., 2020; Riaz et al., 2016; Shah et al., 2021). Amongst metals, Fe has been widely explored due to similar electronic states of Fe and TiO2 which lead to creation of effective e-/h+ trapping sites, leading to higher photocatalytic activity. However, to synthesize best performing photocatalysts different synthesis parameters should be considered including iron loading, synthesis, and calcination temperature. Our previous study reported 0.1Fe-TiO2-300 photocatalyst as the best combination for iron loading and calcination temperature (Khan et al., 2021).

In recent years, GQDs has emerged as a new nanomaterial with remarkable electronic and optical characteristics that can transform systems and devices for energy conversion (Park et al., 2013; Yan et al., 2010). The photo excited GQDs are excellent electron donors and accepters (Tian et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2009). The tunable band gap and up conversion property of GQDs makes them prominent among other semiconductors. The GQDs possess a bandgap of 2.0–3.1 eV despite being derived from zero bandgap graphene (Chen et al., 2018). Since the band gap of the GQDs nanomaterial lies within the visible light spectrum, GQD-TiO2 nanocomposites have attracted considerable attention among the researchers to enhance the charge transport for improved photocatalytic efficiency (Miranda-Andrades et al., 2020; Sarkar et al., 2016). GQDs can greatly increase the decolorization efficiency of TiO2 on methyl orange (MO) dye due to the simultaneous effect of absorption and charge separation of e-/h+ (Qu et al., 2016), while another study showed that GQDs doped with nitrogen and sulfur can effectively improve the degradation potential of TiO2 on MO (Li et al., 2018). According to Chia and Pumera, the combination of graphene with a semiconductor results in the photoexcitation of electrons from valence band to conduction band and are transferred from semiconductor to graphene layers (Chia and Pumera, 2018). Arshad et al. (2018) found the increased efficiency from 43% to 99% of Photo-Fenton process for MO dye upon the addition of graphene synthesized through hydrothermal method. Another research group studied the incremental loading of graphene (0–15%) in Fe3O4 synthesized via co precipitation and found increased efficiency (73%−99%) for phenol degradation (Yu et al., 2016). The role of different carbon supports in MnO prepared through hydrothermal method revealed that the synergistic effect between metal oxide and carbon composite can be achieved with proper textural properties (Ryabova et al., 2017). Furthermore, carbon composite has demonstrated to be effective promoters of electron transfer (Gao and Fang, 2015; Wang et al., 2017; Zhao et al., 2012).

Though much progress has been made on the photocatalysis of metal and nonmetal doped TiO2, there has been little focus on the GQD-TiO2. Combination of GQDs with iron and TiO2 can pave a new horizon in photocatalysis. The main aim of present work was to develop graphene quantum dot and iron Co-doped TiO2 photocatalysts for efficient removal of reactive black 5 (RB5) dye and phytotoxicity assessments upon application of treated water.

The commercial reactive black 5 (RB5), a double azo dye, citric acid (Sigma Aldrich Germany), titanium tetra-isopropoxide (TTIP) 98% (Daejung, South Korea), absolute ethanol (98%), hydrochloric acid (37%), sodium hydro oxide pellets, and acetic acid 99% (Merck Darmstadt, Germany) were purchased and utilized without further purification. Deionized water (D.I) was produced using B114 deionizer in the laboratory.

Citric acid (CA) was used as precursor for the synthesis of GQDs. In a typical method, explained elsewhere (Dong et al., 2012), 2 g of CA was pyrolyzed on a heating mantle (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, USA) at 200 °C for 10 min, the color changed to pale yellow and then turn to orange indicating the development of GQDs. The liquidated GQDs were neutralized with basic ethanol (10 mg mL−1 NaOH) under vigorous stirring.

GQD-Fe-TiO2 photocatalysts were synthesized by employing modified sol gel method (Lee et al., 2012; Ramachandran et al., 2020). Solution A was prepared by adding 37 mL TTIP into 60 mL absolute ethanol while the desired amount of Fe precursor, GQD solution, deionized water, and 15 mL acetic acid were added into 20 mL absolute ethanol to prepare solution B. Solution B was added into solution A under vigorous stirring at room temperature (22 ± 3 °C) until gel formation. The obtained gel was aged for 24 h under ambient conditions, dried in oven (UN 30, Memmert-Germany), and ground to powder. The obtained material was calcined at 300 °C. The calcination temperature and Fe loading were selected as 300 °C and 0.1Fe based on our previous reported study (Khan et al., 2021). In this way 9 different photocatalysts with variable loadings of GQD were synthesize including 0.0GQD-0.1Fe-TiO2-300 (without GQD), 0.2GQD-0.1Fe-TiO2-300, 0.5GQD-0.1Fe-TiO2-300, 1.0GQD-0.1Fe-TiO2-300, 1.5GQD-0.1Fe-TiO2-300, 2.0GQD-0.1Fe-TiO2-300, 3.0GQD-0.1Fe-TiO2-300, 4.0GQD-0.1Fe-TiO2-300 and 5.0GQD-0.1Fe-TiO2-300.

To better understand the physicochemical properties, characterization of the photocatalyst was conducted through various techniques. The information derived can be helpful to enhance the relationship between the physicochemical characteristics and photocatalytic efficiency (Fatimah et al., 2019). Therefore the best-performing photocatalysts were characterized for surface morphology and elemental composition using scanning electron microscopy (SEM) equipped with energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) using a JEOL JSM-6510LA, Tokyo, Japan, X-ray diffraction (XRD-Bruker, Billerica, Massachusetts, United States) was used for phase identification, functional groups through Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR-Alpha Bruker, Karlsruhe, Germany), bandgap estimation using diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS- UV-2600i, Kyoto, Japan) and specific surface area and pores volume by Brunauer–Emmett–Teller analysis (BET- GEMINI VII 2390, Micromeritics Instrument Corp, Norcross, GA, USA). Raman spectroscopy was used for the chemical composition analysis and GQD existence using 1064 nm Fiber Optics. The photoluminescence spectrum (PL) of the synthesized photocatalysts were obtained using FluoroMax-plus-P-C, Horiba Jobin Yvon Technology, USA.

RB5 decolorization studies were conducted under visible light source of 500 W with 30,798 lux light intensity (Halogen lamp, Hi Luminar-Germany,) at 25 cm distance, ambient temperature, 30 mg L−1 of dye concentration, 6.2 pH and 1 g L−1 photocatalyst dose. Initially the GQD-0.1Fe-TiO2-300 photocatalyst was dispersed in distilled water through ultrasonication followed by addition of required amount of RB5 dye. Prior to photocatalytic reaction, the suspension was magnetically stirred for 30 min in dark followed by illumination for 60 min under visible light source. Samples were collected at a pre-determined interval for RB5 adsorption (dark) and decolorization (light).

UV–visible spectrophotometer (PG instruments T80+, Lutterworth, UK) was used to examine the RB5 decolorization at 598 nm. Calibration curve was developed with standard solutions of different RB5 concentrations i.e., 1, 10, 20, 30, 50, 60 and 100 mg L−1. The suspended particles of photocatalysts were removed through centrifugation each time prior to the absorbance measurement. RB5 decolorization (%) was determined using the Eq. (1).

Where C0 and Ct represent the RB5 concentration, respectively, before and after irradiation at time t. Moreover, effect of different reaction parameters including the contact time, pH, Fe-TiO2 dose and initial dye concentration on RB5 decolorization were investigated with the same experimental setup and procedure explained above.

The active species trapping studies were conducted in RB5 decolorization experiments under similar conditions. Scavengers were introduced to reaction solution prior the addition of the photocatalysts in decolorization system. Isopropyl alcohol (IPA) was used as hydroxyl radical (•OH), AgNO3 as an electron and di sodium ethylenediaminetetraacetate (EDTA) as hole (h+) scavengers.

In the photocatalytic process, dye adsorption is a crucial and significant factor. Adsorption studies were carried out in dark (without light). The experimental data of RB5 adsorption were analyzed through well-established isotherms, namely Freundlich and Langmuir. The linear transformation of Langmuir isotherm (Eq. (2)) was used to calculate the value of Qm and Kads, the plot of 1/Qe and 1/Ce is constructed where Kads can be derived from the slope (1/Qm Kads).

Qe (mg·g−1) is the amount of dye adsorbed per unit weight of the photocatalyst at the equilibrium, Qm (mg·g−1) is the maximal adsorbed quantity, Ce (mg·L−1) indicate the residual concentration of the RB5 at equilibrium and Kads (L·mg−1) is Langmuir adsorption constant. KF [mg·g−1(L·mg−1)1/n] and n are the Freundlich constants related to the adsorption capacity and adsorption intensity, respectively.

Langmuir-Hinshelwood (L-H) model (Houas, 2001) was employed to elaborate the rate of the photocatalytic decolorization of RB5 dye over time. L-H model for photocatalytic system can be explained as:

The dependency of 1/r0 for the corresponding 1/[RB5]e concentration values of RB5 can be translated by Eq. (4). In comparison, the kc and KLH values demonstrate the effect of the RB5 concentration on the equilibrium constant.

A standardized protocol (ISO/DIS 15799, (1999)) was used to perform phytotoxicity experiments (Apostol et al., 2015). L. esculentum was used as phytotoxicity assessment indicator. The tolerance of the seed germination to GQD-0.1Fe-TiO2-300 photocatalyst and treated water was measured to assess the toxicity. The seeds were provided by Department of Biotechnology, COMSATS University Islamabad, Abbottabad campus. The physically damaged and abnormal seed were separated and removed using a magnifier. Toxicological experiments were conducted for TiO2-300, 0.1Fe-TiO2-300 and GQD-0.1Fe-TiO2-300 photocatalysts as well as water treated with these photocatalysts. Simultaneously, control experiment was also run using RB5 dye solution. Germination (%) was determined using the following Eq. (5).

Where GS is germinated seeds and TS is total number of seeds.

Recycle studies were performed on the used TiO2-300, 0.1Fe-TiO2-300 and GQD-0.1Fe-TiO2 300 photocatalysts. The used-photocatalysts washed and centrifuged with deionized water and dried at 80 °C overnight. After drying the same amount of the recovered photocatalyst was reused under the same experimental condition as described above and decolorization (%) was determined using the Eq. (1).

Energy efficiency was estimated through electrical energy consumption (EE/O) for TiO2-300 and GQD-0.1Fe-TiO2-300 photocatalysts using the following Eq. (6) (Azbar et al., 2004).

Fig. 1a, b indicates the SEM analysis of 0.1Fe-TiO2-300 and GQD-0.1Fe-TiO2-300. 0.1Fe-TiO2-300 depicted the spherical morphologies with uniform distribution, however, upon doping with GQDs dispersed particles can be seen on the surface of substrate. Fig. 1c shows the EDS spectrum of GQD-0.1Fe-TiO2-300. Previous studies support our observation that the morphology of GQDs cannot be clearly determined even at higher magnification level (Shen et al., 2017; Tian et al., 2017). Moreover, localized metal particles were not detected indicating high iron dispersion on the surface of TiO2. This observation agrees with the results of XRD, the presence of Fe metal was not found in XRD pattern, this can be due to the smaller crystal size of the iron and good dispersion of these species onto the titanium surface, similar observation were reported previously for different Fe doped TiO2 photocatalysts (Šijaković-Vuji et al., 2004; Wantala et al., 2010). The average particle size determined for GQD-0.1Fe-TiO2-300 was found to be 20 nm, while 40 nm average particle size was reported in our previous study for 0.1Fe-TiO2-300 (Khan et al., 2021).

The XRD analysis were conducted to check the effect of dopants (Fe and GQD) on the crystallinity of the TiO2. Fig. 2a shows XRD pattern of synthesized TiO2 and GQD-0.1Fe-TiO2-300 photocatalysts. The results are compared with the reference (anatase TiO2) and change in FWHM was recorded. FWHM of the main diffraction peak of anatase TiO2 (25.3°) increased from 0.23075° to 1.16357° when doped with Fe (Khan et al., 2021) and 1.5987° upon doping with GQD and 0.1Fe. Vashista and Paul (2012) reported that the XRD peaks broaden as stacking defects and structural disorder develop. Moreover, an increase in hardness and density of point defects impacts the crystallinity and grain boundary mobility, resulting a linear increase in the FWHM of XRD peak (Vashista and Paul, 2012). The diffraction peaks are well assigned to the anatase TiO2 crystalline phase (JCPDS 84–1286), no peaks were detected for the rutile and brookite phases in TiO2, and GQDs decorated Fe doped TiO2 photocatalysts. It can be seen in all samples that the anatase crystalline peak maintained in all samples and the addition of GQD and 0.1Fe does not affect the crystalline phase (anatase) of TiO2. No diffraction peaks of carbon species were observed due to the low concentration of the GQDs (Shen et al., 2017). Moreover, the radius sizes of Ti4+ (0.68 Å) and Fe3+(0.64 Å) were almost similar, it may be assumed that the Fe ions inserted into the structure of titania and occupied part of the lattice spaces of TiO2 (Zhou et al., 2005). Our results are comparable with previous studies (Abdelhaleem et al., 2019; Abdelhaleem and Chu, 2017; Riaz, 2011) for low-temperature impregnation process of TiO2.

FTIR analysis was used to understand the structures of the nanomaterial before and after GQDs and Fe addition. Fig. 2b illustrated the FTIR spectra of TiO2-300, 0.1Fe-TiO2-300 and GQD-0.1Fe-TiO2-300. In all the synthesized nanocomposites, the peaks around 400–900 cm−1 range are attributed to Ti-O-Ti bonds, (Teymourinia et al., 2019). Furthermore, peaks at 1520, 1740 and 3525 cm−1 in GQD-0.1Fe-TiO2-300 nanocomposite can be assigned to the C-H, CC and O-H functional groups, respectively. Vibrational absorption band of oxygen containing functional groups like carbonyl (-CO) and epoxy (-C-O-C-) were assigned at 1757 cm−1 and 1120 cm−1, respectively (Ebrahimi et al., 2020; Tian et al., 2017), moreover, the peak around 2131 cm−1 correspond to C-O-Ti bond (C-O at oxygen deficient sites in TiO2) (Liao et al., 2002). The presence of GQDs in the hybrid sample is confirmed by these findings. In addition, the inset of Fig. 2d demonstrates no visible difference in GQD-0.1Fe-TiO2-300 before and after RB5 decolorization.

The task of allowing TiO2 to operate in the visible light source is to modify its lattice structure by adding some impurities. Diffuse reflectance spectroscopy was used to observe this shift and estimate the bandgap. Fig. 2c shows the absorption spectrum of TiO2-300 and GQD-0.1Fe-TiO2-300 photocatalysts. A visible-light absorption was noticed for GQD-0.1Fe-TiO2-300 compared to TiO2-300, however difference between 0.1Fe-TiO2-300 and GQD-0.1Fe-TiO2-300 was not marked as reported in our previous study (Khan et al., 2021). The sharp absorption edge at 390 nm has been assigned to the excitation of the electron from VB to CB in previous studies (Cheng et al., 2016) and the excitation range is confirmed through PL studies in the preceding section of this manuscript. In addition, from the plot of (F(R) hv)1/2 versus hv, the photocatalysts' bandgap energy was calculated. The photocatalyst’s bandgap yielded by extrapolating it to the tangent of the graph in the low energy range (hv) axis when [F(R) hv]1/2 = 0 as shown in Fig. 2d. The bandgap values for TiO2-300 and GQD-0.1Fe-TiO2-300 photocatalysts were found to be 3.20, and 2.94 eV, respectively. Previous studies demonstrated the role of graphene in narrowing the band gap (Tian et al., 2017), however, the comparative analysis of iron and graphene doped TiO2 through DRS and PL spectrum showed less variation in the alteration of bandgap, so the higher photocatalytic activity of GQD-0.1Fe-TiO2-300 can be ascribed to the light sensitizing effect of graphene.

BET results showed the surface area of 170.79 m2 g−1 and pore volume of 0.08 cm3 g−1 for GQD-0.1Fe-TiO2-300 while in our previous study the surface area reported for 0.1Fe-TiO2-300 was 70 m2 g−1 and pore volume was found to be 0.115 cm3 g−1 (Khan et al., 2021). The surface area increased upon loading with GQDs, this fact can also be observed in dark reaction studies, where the adsorption of RB5 dye onto GQDs loaded photocatalyst was exhibited higher compared to unloaded photocatalysts. Results from adsorption studies best fit with Langmuir isotherm showing the formation of monolayer chemisorption mechanism, which agree with N2 adsorption desorption analysis. In a previous study, focusing on increasing the GQD loadings from 1.5% to 3.5% resulted in a slight increment in surface area from 123 to 130 m2 g−1 (Qu et al., 2016). Moreover, our results can be compared with a previous study, wherein 2.5 wt percent GQD-TiO2 synthesized with hydrothermal and ultrasonic synthesis method depicted the surface area of 115.75 and 133.32 m2 g−1 respectively (Shafaee et al., 2018).

Fig. 3 presents PL spectra of TiO2-300, 0.1Fe-TiO2-300 and GQD-0.1Fe-TiO2-300. In a semiconductor the PL occurs due to the electron and hole recombination and the intensity of PL is directly proportional to the rate of electron and hole recombination (Stankovich et al., 2006). The PL exhibits excitation-dependent properties of the synthesized material, the broad emission for all samples occurred between the range of 330–550 nm, this is attributed to the self -trapping excitons localized on TiO6 octahedra and oxygen vacancies (Aleksandrzak et al., 2015; Lu et al., 2013). In the current case, this indicates that doping of Fe and GQDs does not change the mechanism of TiO2 photocatalysis. Moreover, the intensity of PL spectra is significantly reduced in 0.1Fe-TiO2 and GQD-0.1Fe-TiO2-300 compared to TiO2-300, indicating the elongation of the e-/h+ pairs lifetime. Furthermore, almost similar PL intensity was observed for GQD, and Fe doped TiO2, demonstrating that GQDs have no influence on the charge recombination of the TiO2 but do have light sensitizing effect which increased the photocatalytic activity of GQD-0.1Fe-TiO2-300. These results support the DRS findings where minor change was exhibited between the band gap of 0.1Fe-TiO2 and GQD-0.1Fe-TiO2-300 photocatalysts.

Fig. 4a shows the Raman spectra of the synthesized photocatalysts, characteristic Raman band (Eg) of anatase TiO2 was displayed in all three photocatalysts whereas major Raman feature of D band was revealed at around 1370 cm−1 and a smaller G band at 1588 cm−1. Earlier studies showed that the in-plane (Eg (2)) vibration of sp2 hybridized carbon atoms come from the Raman G band, while the D band originates from the edge states in graphene (Rajender et al., 2018). Based on the intensity of the bands, the type of edge arrangements can be determined. The existence of D band in GQDs indicates armchair edges, while no D band appears in zig zag edges of GQDs (Bischoff et al., 2011; Casiraghi et al., 2009). In addition, the shift of the Eg (2) Raman mode in the doped photocatalysts explains the connection of oxygen-deficient TiO2 through the probable C-O-Ti bonds (Rajender et al., 2018). Fig. 4b depicted the comparison of Eg (2) peak profiles of the pristine TiO2 and doped TiO2, a prominent shift 17 and 56 cm−1 was observed for Fe-GQD-TiO2 and Fe-TiO2 photocatalysts.

Experiments were performed using optimized Fe-TiO2 (0.1Fe-TiO2-300) from our previous study (Khan et al., 2021) with different GQDs loading as 0.0, 0.2, 0.5, 1, 1.5, 2.0, 3.0, 4.0 and 5.0 wt percent. Fig. 5a depicts increase in percent RB5 decolorization with increase in GQDs content till an optimum loading. 97.40% RB5 decolorization was obtained for 1GQD-0.1Fe-TiO2-300. The increase in RB5 decolorization with increase in GQDs loading can be due to increment in photoabsorption. In a photocatalytic reaction, graphene acts as a photosensitizer, absorb electron from photoinduced irradiation and transfer them to the TiO2 (Park et al., 2013), in this way the photocatalyst can generate more electrons and holes and ultimately more superoxide and hydroxyl radicals available for the decolorization of dye. Further increase in the GQDs loading resulted in lower photocatalytic activity, which is probably due to light scattering resulted from the increased dose of GQDs which in turn decreased the absorption of incident light and reduced the catalytic active sites (Xie et al., 2017; Yu et al., 2014). Moreover, in our case the trapping experiments and mechanism studies revealed the possibility of photon absorption by Fe-TiO2 and then transfer to GQDs as the VB of Fe-TiO2 is higher than VB of GQDs which could transfer electron to VB of GQDs. These findings are in agreement with Shende et al. (2018), where they found the optimum GQDs loading of 4 mg for decolorization of crystal violet dye. Fig. 5a shows that Fe-TiO2-300 has lower photocatalytic activity compared to GQDs loaded Fe-TiO2, which support our argument that the presence of GQDs increase the photoabsorption and photodecolorization. The optimized ratio of GQD:Fe: TiO2 was found to be 10:1:89 for the best synthesized photocatalyst, i.e., GQD-0.1Fe-TiO2 calcined at 300 °C.

The optimization of GQD-0.1Fe-TiO2-300 dose is a significant concern that is important in preventing the use of excessive nanomaterial and ensures effective photon absorption. The general acceptance is that the decolorization efficiency increases with increase in dose until an optimum dose level, afterwards RB5 decolorization would be constant or start declining with further increase in photocatalyst dose. Fig. 5b shows the effect of different GQD-0.1Fe-TiO2-300 dose on the RB5 decolorization. In control experiment, only light and without addition of photocatalyst, a very little decolorization (1.1%) was observed, but it increased from 50% to 97.40% when the dose of GQD-0.1Fe-TiO2-300 augmented from 0.25 to 1.0 mg mL−1, respectively. Moreover, upon further enhancing the photocatalyst dose from 1.0 to 8.0 mg mL−1 the decolorization efficiency decreased from 97.40% to 45.30%, respectively. Such findings are typical for most of the photocatalytic reactions. Increasing in dose results in availability of more active sites on the GQD-0.1Fe-TiO2-300 surface and hence increase the RB5 decolorization, however, further increment in dose increase the turbidity of the reaction medium and result in the scattering of the light irradiation (Ashraf et al., 2020; Iftikhar et al., 2020; Kurniawan et al., 2020). Some research studies reported the reduced surface area due to the agglomeration of the photocatalysts responsible for lower decolorization at higher dose (Bhatt and Patel, 2021; Singh and Dhaliwal, 2020).

In photocatalytic process, pH is one of the influencing factors in decolorization of the organic pollutants in wastewater treatment system. Fig. 5c demonstrates the effect of variable pH on the RB5 decolorization efficiency of GQD-0.1Fe-TiO2-300. Decolorization efficiency does not change noticeably between pH 2–6. However, lower RB5 decolorization (35.60%) was observed at pH 12. Acidic pH is more favorable in decolorization of RB5 dye using GQD-0.1Fe-TiO2-300 compared to basic pH. At lower pH the GQD-0.1Fe-TiO2-300 surface is positively charged, RB5 is an anionic dye possessing negative charge due to dissociation of sulfonic groups, resulting in repulsion between the RB5 dye molecules and GQD-0.1Fe-TiO2-300, that in turn cause negligible adsorption at higher pH and strong attraction at lower pH, could be favored leading to high photodecolorization of the RB5 dye (Bhatt and Patel, 2021). Similar results were reported in previous studies for anionic dyes and the higher photocatalytic decolorization of RB5 at acidic pH (Tafurt-García et al., 2018; Sadhu et al., 2020).

The impact of initial concentration of RB5 dye on the decolorization efficiency of GQD-0.1Fe- TiO2-300 photocatalyst was studied by varying concentrations i.e., 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60 and 100 mg L−1. The results are provided separately in supplementary materials as Fig. S1. It was found that the decolorization efficiency of GQD-0.1Fe- TiO2-300 initially increase from 90% to 99% up to 60 mg L−1 and then decrease to 65% for 100 mg L−1 of initial RB5 concentration. For quantitative evaluation, the RB5 decolorization data was fitted to first, pseudo first order (PFO) and second order (SO) kinetic models, the plot of these kinetic models are given in supplementary material as Fig.S2, Fig. S3, and Fig. S4. Kinetic data best fitted to the PFO model, therefore, the photocatalytic decolorization reaction of RB5 by GQD-0.1Fe-TiO2-300 belongs to the PFO reaction kinetics. The rate constants (K) are depicted in Table 1 for PFO and SO kinetic models.

Study of the dependence of photocatalytic reaction rate on the pollutant concentration is important from mechanistic and an application point of view. It is generally observed that the decolorization rate increases with the increment in dye concentration to a certain level and a further increase in dye concentration leads to the decrease in decolorization rate. Fig. 5d depicted the rate of RB5 decolorization for different initial RB5 concentration. The rate increased from 1.06 min−1 at 10 mg mL−1 to 2.47 min−1 at 60 mg L−1 and then declined to 1.2 min−1 at 200 mg mL−1. These results show that higher decolorization efficiency can be achieved with lower RB5 concentration using GQD-0.1Fe- TiO2-300 photocatalysts.

The higher efficiency and decolorization rate at lower initial RB5 dye concentration can be explained by the availability of active sites for adsorption and photocatalytic activity, the gradual increase in RB5 concentration occupies the active sites, thus reduces the photocatalytic performance (Haque and Muneer, 2007; Reza et al., 2017). Moreover, photons are blocked by intense color in the solution and dye molecules adsorbed on to the surface of GQD-0.1Fe- TiO2-300 photocatalysts, this leads to lower •OH and •O2 species generation (Dariani et al., 2016; Kiwaan et al., 2020).

L-H kinetic model was used to determine the kinetics of photocatalytic reaction., Fig. 6 (inset) confirms the L-H relationship by plotting the 1/Kapp vs [RB5]o. The KC value was found to be 2.45 mg L−1 min−1 while the KLH value was observed as 0.45 L mg−1. The higher KC value corresponds to the dominant photocatalytic process compared to the adsorption process (Riaz et al., 2020; Shah et al., 2021; Valente et al., 2006). The results from L-H model also depicts that heterogenous photocatalytic process involves the adsorption and photocatalytic processes and proceed simultaneously (Khan et al., 2020). Moreover, the concurrent process was confirmed in Fig. 6 as the rate of photodecolorization increased with increase in initial RB5 concentration (100 mg L−1) until the active sites are occupied by the higher RB5 concentration. Furthermore, the theoretical evaluation of the photodecolorization of the studied RB5 concentrations can be obtained from the L-H kinetic model fitness using the Eq. (5) below:

To better understand the contribution of adsorption in decolorization of RB5, adsorption studies (dark reaction without light) were conducted for GQD-0.1Fe-TiO2-300. The distribution of the dye between solid and liquid phase is determined by the adsorption isotherms (Langmuir and Freundlich). The adsorption capacities at equilibrium were reported as a function of time and initial concentration are depicted in Fig. 7a, and Fig. 7b. The adsorption RB5 was slower and equilibrium time was reached after 100 min. Our findings are comparable with previous studies reporting the equilibrium time during adsorption of dye in 90 min (Fraga et al., 2020) and 300 min (da Silva et al., 2021).

To understand the mechanism, the adsorption data was fitted into Langmuir and Freundlich isotherm models. Langmuir isotherm (plot 1/Qe vs 1/Ce) is depicted in Fig. 8a. The Qm and Kads for GQD-0.1Fe-TiO2-300 are found to be 56.49 mg g−1 and 0.046 L mg −1 respectively. The plot of Freundlich isotherm (lnQe vs lnCe) depicted in Fig. 8b showed the KF and 1/n, respectively, as 26.03 L g−1 and 0.7065. Despite the low coefficient of determination R2 for the Freundlich isotherm, i.e., 0.9865 compared to that of Langmuir (0.9976), the value of the heterogeneity factor (1/n) was determined to be less (0.70 in this study) than 1, implying the existence of physisorption of RB5 onto the GQD-0.1Fe-TiO2-300 photocatalyst which reveals the co-existence of some heterogeneous surfaces on GQD-0.1Fe-TiO2-300.

UV Visible spectrum recorded at different time intervals for RB5 dye is depicted in Fig. 9. Three main bands were observed initially at 0 min, including i) the main RB5 band at 598 nm, ii) naphthalene compounds at 288 nm, and iii) benzene band at 244 nm. The intensity of the RB5 representative peak drastically reduced after 10 min of the photocatalytic reaction under visible light irradiation. After 60 min of irradiation complete decolorization for RB5 dye was observed. The disappearance of the main peak is attributed to the fragmentation of azo links by oxidation.

The band gap, separation efficiency of photogenerated electrons and holes, and oxidation potential of photogenerated holes are all well-known factors that influence photocatalytic activity. In general, the photocatalytic elimination of organic pollutants is carried out by three primary reactive species (hole, •OH radical, and electron). The crucial factor among these factors is the charge separation at the interface of a photocatalyst. To study the migration path of the photogenerated electrons and holes in GQD-0.1Fe-TiO2-300 photocatalyst, the radical-trapping experiments were conducted employing isopropyl alcohol (IPA) as hydroxyl radical (•OH), AgNO3 as an electron and di sodium ethylenediaminetetraacetate (EDTA) as hole (h+) scavengers. Fig. 10 shows that the photodecolorization of RB5 is not much affected by the addition of AgNO3, indicating almost no or very little involvement of superoxide radicals (•O2 -) or the photogenerated electrons. The addition of IPA reduced the photocatalytic activity to 63% after 60 min of visible light irradiation, revealed the existence of •OH radical species. In addition, the photocatalytic activity was thoroughly suppressed (46%) upon addition of EDTA as a hole scavenger, indicating the holes are the major pathway in the process of RB5 degradation. These results indicated that although e-, h+ and •OH are active components in the photocatalytic system but the h+ and •OH are main active species for the photodecolorization of RB5 using GQD-0.1Fe-TiO2-300 photocatalysts.

Based on obtained results during trapping experiments and characterization, the possible reaction mechanism for the degradation of RB5 dye is proposed to explain the enhanced photocatalytic activity as depicted in Fig. 11. The potential of conduction band (CB) and valence band (VB) were calculated using the following empirical equations.

a, b, and c are the number of atoms in the compounds (Yuan et al., 2014). The values of Eg and, χ for Fe-TiO2 and GQD are 4.76 and 6.26 eV, respectively. Therefore, the ECB of Fe-TiO2 and GQD were −1.2 and 0.36 eV respectively while EVB for Fe-TiO2 and GQD was 1.73 and 3.16 eV versus normal hydrogen electrode (NHE). When the GQD-Fe-TiO2 nanocomposite is irradiated with visible light electrons are transferred from the valance band of GQDs to either CB of GQDs or the VB of TiO2. Due to high energy level of CB of TiO2, the electrons are transferred from CBTiO2 to CBGQDs. Moreover, the energy level of CBTiO2 is also high, therefore, there is a higher possibility that electrons from VBTiO2 are transferred to VBGQDs. The lifetime of the charge carrier is prolonged in this way resulting in the reduction of charge recombination, which is favorable for higher photocatalytic activity. In addition, based on the trapping experiments, the electrons in the CB can be involved in the reduction reaction to form H2O2, which results in formation of OH radicals and ultimately RB5 decolorization. Holes in the VB are reported to be directly involved in the decolorization of dye into byproducts (Kumar et al., 2018). Similar charge transfer pathways were proposed in the previous studies on Ti3+-TiO2/GQDs (Tang et al., 2018) and S-GQDs/TiO2 (Luo et al., 2018).

Toxicity tests were conducted to evaluate the phytotoxicity of TiO2-300, 0.1Fe-TiO2-300, and GQD-0.1Fe-TiO2-300 photocatalysts and the treated water using an indirect toxicity bioassay on the L. esculentum seed germination according to the standard procedure. Experiments were also performed for control (only RB5 dye). The seed germination is depicted in Fig. 12. All three types of the photocatalyst had very little effect on seed germination, TiO2-300, 0.1Fe-TiO2-300 displayed 67% and 73% seed germination while addition of GQDs to the photocatalysts improved seed germination to 81.43%. The treated water with GQD-0.1Fe-TiO2-300 showed higher seed germination 69% while water treated with TiO2-300 and 0.1Fe-TiO2-300 had lower seed germination. Control experiments with only RB5 showed lower seed germination of 46% compared to treated as well as photocatalysts containing water. Similar results were reported for RB5 and other reactive dyes on the growth rate of mung beans (Khan and Malik, 2018).

One of the principal goals of heterogeneous photocatalysis is the reusability or recyclability of the photocatalyst. Unfortunately, this aspect of photocatalysis has not got much attention. In fact, a photocatalyst must consistently be capable of withstanding the reaction conditions multiple times for it to be industrially viable. The performance of the photocatalyst must not alter dramatically upon reusing. Heterogeneous catalysts are usually observed to undergo deactivation or loss of activity predominantly due to catalytic poison. The ease of recovery and reliability in terms of performance over the number of cycles are highly desirable properties when synthesizing a photocatalyst.

The recyclability of the synthesized photocatalysts up to four cycles is depicted in Fig. 13. Initially in first cycle (C1) the decolorization efficiency for TiO2-300, 0.1Fe-TiO2-300 and GQD-0.1Fe-TiO2-300 was 45%, 91% and 97% respectively, while in second cycle (C2) it was reduced to 39%, 80% and 79% respectively. Almost, 50% reduction in decolorization efficiency for RB5 dye was observed in the performance of 0.1Fe-TiO2-300 and GQD-0.1Fe-TiO2-300 photocatalysts in 4th cycle (C4). The lower performance after 4th cycle could be due to the adsorption of the intermediate like aliphatic hydrocarbons. Previous studies have reported that the reduced photocatalytic activity during recycling could be due to the loss of active sites upon multiple re-uses and washing (El-Mekkawi et al., 2020). Moreover, these results indicate the stability of, GQD-0.1Fe-TiO2-300 photocatalyst to the reaction conditions. Similar results were found for TiO2 @S-AZMB after two cycles (Zhao et al., 2020). Based on these results it can be concluded that GQD-0.1Fe-TiO2-300 photocatalyst can be used for multiple cycles.

Table 2 compares the energy consumption (energy efficiency EE/O) and price estimation for the removal of different pollutants (dyes) in 1000 L of wastewater. The energy consumption for bare TiO2 was found higher compared to 0.1Fe-TiO2-300 and GQD-0.1Fe-TiO2-300. The energy consumption for 0.1Fe-TiO2-300 were reported to be 207 K W h m−3 in our previous study (Khan et al., 2021) and the energy consumption was reduced to 137.47 K W h m−3 for GQD-0.1Fe-TiO2-300. Based on the energy consumption it can be concluded that GQD-0.1Fe-TiO2-300 is best for the decolorization of RB5 double azo dye, which not only reduced the cost of energy but also reduced the time for the decolorization of RB5 dye.

GQD-0.1Fe-TiO2-300 photocatalyst was ranked as an efficient heterogenous photocatalyst for decolorization of RB5 dye, a representative pollutant from textile wastewater. Screening studies showed GQDs content highly influenced the photocatalytic decolorization and the best selected combination was pH 6.2, 1 mg mL−1 dose, 60 mg L−1 concentration and ambient temperature (22 ± 3 °C). The illumination reaction followed the PFO reaction with the highest Kapp of 2.4 mg min−1. Moreover, the reaction followed the L-H kinetic expression of heterogenous photocatalysis, having higher KC value (2.45 mg L−1 min−1) as compared to KLH value (0.45 L mg−1). In the reported photocatalyst, i.e., GQD-0.1Fe-TiO2-300, the holes and OH radicals played active role in the decolorization of RB5 dye. Moreover, both GQDs and 0.1Fe accelerated the photon energy harnessing capability of TiO2. GQD-0.1Fe-TiO2-300 was found durable up to four cycles and energy efficient as compared to undoped photocatalysts. Simultaneously with total decolorization, no adverse effects were found on the seed germination of L. esculentum. This research work could pave the way for further investigation into photocatalytic and phytotoxic features of graphene quantum dots alone and in combination with other metal/nonmetal doped photocatalysts.
